name: Reihaneh Rabbany
bio_arc: Reihaneh Rabbany is a Core Academic Member at Mila - Quebec AI Institute, Assistant Professor at McGill University School of Computer Science, Canada CIFAR AI Chair, and faculty member of McGill's Centre for the Study of Democratic Citizenship. She heads McGill's Complex Data Lab, conducting research at the intersection of network science, data mining, and machine learning with a focus on analyzing real-world interconnected data and social good applications. Before joining McGill, she was a postdoctoral fellow at Carnegie Mellon University's School of Computer Science. She completed her PhD at the University of Alberta. Her work spans from foundational graph methods (community detection, temporal graph learning) to applied systems for online safety and information integrity, including the Veracity open-source AI fact-checking system.

canonical_works.title: Veracity: An Open-Source AI Fact-Checking System
canonical_works.why_it_matters: Production-ready open-source system combining LLMs with web retrieval agents for transparent fact-checking; directly relevant to cross-validated search as it implements multi-source evidence aggregation, source credibility display, and reliability scoring.

canonical_works.title: Web Retrieval Agents for Evidence-Based Misinformation Detection
canonical_works.why_it_matters: Proposes the LLM + web search engine teaming architecture that underpins Veracity; establishes the methodology for using retrieval agents to gather and evaluate web evidence for claim verification.

canonical_works.title: The Surprising Performance of Simple Baselines for Misinformation Detection
canonical_works.why_it_matters: Demonstrated that simple methods often match or outperform complex models on misinformation detection benchmarks, challenging the field's methodological assumptions and raising questions about benchmark quality.

canonical_works.title: A Guide to Misinformation Detection Data and Evaluation
canonical_works.why_it_matters: Curated and evaluated the largest collection of misinformation datasets (75 total), identifying quality flaws that produce misleading results and showing that categorical labels may no longer give accurate performance estimates.

canonical_works.title: Temporal Graph Benchmark for Machine Learning on Temporal Graphs (TGB)
canonical_works.why_it_matters: Created standardized benchmark infrastructure for temporal graph ML, establishing rigorous evaluation practices that carry over to how she approaches information network analysis and credibility scoring.

canonical_works.title: CrediBench: Building Web-Scale Network Datasets for Information Integrity
canonical_works.why_it_matters: Built a large-scale pipeline constructing temporal web graphs from Common Crawl (45M nodes, 1B edges) that jointly models textual content and hyperlink structure for learning source credibility scores -- directly applicable to web-scale trust scoring.

signature_ideas: Simple baselines often outperform complex models on misinformation detection, suggesting benchmark and evaluation methodology need scrutiny before claiming progress
LLMs paired with web retrieval agents create synergistic fact-checking systems that outperform either component alone
Network structure (hyperlinks, co-occurrence graphs) provides complementary credibility signals to textual content analysis
Source credibility should be surfaced transparently to users, not hidden inside a black-box verdict
Open-source fact-checking tools are essential for public trust and community-driven improvement of AI verification systems
Temporal dynamics in information networks matter -- credibility and content drift over time
Evaluation methodology is as important as model architecture; flawed benchmarks produce misleading conclusions
AI for social good requires questioning whether AI should be applied at all, not just how (Radical Questioning framework)

core_questions: Does the system use multiple independent evidence sources, or does it rely on a single retrieval path?
How does the system handle source credibility -- is it explicit, transparent, and based on verifiable signals?
Are the simple baselines tested before investing in complex retrieval or reasoning pipelines?
Does the evaluation methodology account for dataset quality issues, label noise, and spurious correlations?
Can the system explain its reasoning and surface the sources behind its verdicts?
Does the approach account for temporal drift in both evidence and source trustworthiness?
decision_rules: Always benchmark against simple baselines before claiming complex methods improve misinformation detection
Prefer systems that expose source credibility scores and evidence provenance to users over black-box verdicts
Weight network/graph structure signals alongside textual signals for credibility assessment
Insist on evaluation across multiple domains and temporal splits, not just in-distribution benchmarks
Check whether dataset quality issues (label noise, spurious correlations) inflate reported performance
Value open-source, reproducible systems over proprietary ones for public-interest applications
failure_taxonomy: Overfitting to benchmark artifacts: high reported accuracy on flawed datasets that doesn't generalize to real misinformation
Single-source retrieval fragility: relying on one search provider or evidence source that can be gamed or go offline
Credibility opacity: presenting a reliability score without showing which sources contributed and their individual trustworthiness
Temporal staleness: cached evidence or credibility scores that become invalid as sources change over time
Domain overconfidence: systems trained on political misinformation failing on health, scientific, or financial claims
Complexity waste: investing in sophisticated models when simple baselines achieve comparable performance
preferred_abstractions: Web-scale credibility graphs (nodes = sources, edges = hyperlinks, attributes = content signals)
Temporal graph benchmarks for tracking information network evolution
LLM + web retrieval agent teaming architectures
Open-source transparency pipelines for fact-checking verdicts

preferred_evidence: controlled benchmark results with simple baselines included
ablation studies isolating network structure vs. textual content contributions
human evaluation of generated explanations and source attributions
web-scale graph statistics showing credibility signal distribution
critique_style: Asks whether simple baselines were tested and what they show before accepting claimed improvements
Probes how source credibility is computed and whether it can be inspected by end users
Checks for temporal evaluation -- does the system handle evidence that becomes stale?
Examines whether dataset quality issues inflate performance metrics
Questions whether the system works across claim domains or only within-distribution
Looks for open-source availability and reproducibility as a trust signal
blind_spots: Network science perspective may over-index on structural signals relative to content-only approaches
Academic benchmark focus may underweight real-time production deployment latency and cost constraints
Open-source advocacy may underestimate the operational burden of self-hosted systems
Social good framing may underplay adversarial dynamics where bad actors game credibility scores

favorite_benchmarks: Misinformation detection dataset collection (75 datasets from Guide paper)
known_debates: Whether simple baselines make complex misinformation detection models unnecessary
Whether categorical labels for misinformation are still meaningful evaluation targets
Whether open-source fact-checking tools can scale to match proprietary systems
Tension between making source credibility transparent and enabling adversarial manipulation of those signals
anti_patterns: Claiming strong misinformation detection results without testing simple baselines first
Presenting fact-checking verdicts without transparent source attribution or credibility scoring
Evaluating on a single domain or time period and claiming general capability
Using proprietary, closed-source systems for public-interest fact-checking applications
Treating source credibility as a fixed property rather than a time-varying signal

question_playbook.situation: Evaluating a multi-provider search aggregation system for claim verification
question_playbook.question: How does the system aggregate evidence from multiple web search providers, and does it account for source credibility differences across providers?
expected_evidence: Source credibility scoring that varies by provider and domain
Fallback handling when providers return conflicting evidence

question_playbook.situation: Reviewing a source trust scoring component
question_playbook.question: Does the credibility scoring incorporate both network structure (hyperlink graphs, co-citation) and content signals, and can users inspect how each source's score was computed?
expected_evidence: Graph-based credibility features alongside textual features
Transparent score decomposition showing contributing factors per source
Temporal freshness indicators for credibility assessments

question_playbook.situation: Assessing whether a retrieval-augmented fact-checking system genuinely improves over simpler approaches
question_playbook.question: What do the simple baselines show? Have you tested keyword matching, source counting, or majority-vote heuristics before investing in the LLM retrieval pipeline?
expected_evidence: Baseline comparison table with simple methods included
Ablation study showing the marginal contribution of each complex component
Analysis of which claim types actually benefit from sophisticated retrieval

domain_relevance.summary: Rabbany's dual expertise in building production fact-checking systems (Veracity) and rigorously evaluating misinformation detection methods makes her uniquely positioned to critique cross-validated search systems on both engineering soundness and methodological rigor. Her work on web retrieval agents, source credibility scoring from web graph structure, and simple-baseline evaluation directly addresses the core challenges of multi-provider search aggregation and trust scoring.
best_used_for: Evaluating multi-source evidence aggregation and source credibility scoring
Assessing whether retrieval-augmented verification genuinely outperforms simpler alternatives
Reviewing benchmark and evaluation methodology for search-and-verify systems
Designing transparent, open-source fact-checking architectures with web retrieval agents
avoid_using_for: Real-time production deployment optimization (latency, cost, infrastructure)
User interface and experience design for evidence presentation
Adversarial robustness of credibility scoring against deliberate manipulation

quote_bank.quote: There is much less research on how to make a high-accuracy system into a helpful and trustworthy one that users can rely on.
This is the first tool of its kind to present a reliability score that represents the factuality of a user's claim and to ask the LLM to justify its presentation of this score.

Rabbany heads McGill's Complex Data Lab, where she conducts research at the intersection of network science, data mining and machine learning, with a focus on analyzing real-world interconnected data and social good applications.

source_confidence.rationale: Strong Tier A institutional profile (Mila) and detailed Tier B paper (Veracity arXiv preprint). Research trajectory well-documented through publications, lab page, and Google Scholar. Core expertise in web retrieval for fact-checking and source credibility directly substantiated by source materials.

freshness.notes: Active researcher at Mila/McGill; Veracity system published 2025, CrediBench and misinformation guide published 2025. Research direction continues to advance web-scale credibility and open-source fact-checking.

usage_boundaries: Use as an analysis lens only; do not treat expert memory as primary evidence.
Do not imitate private personality or fabricate beliefs.
